coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Co(II) in actual water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuqi
Liu, Nian
Huang, Jiaoyan
Wang, Suiping
Qing, Taiping
Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: This study focuses on the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) using a hydrothermal method, and its application as a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode probe for simple and rapid detection of Co(II). When Co(II) reacts with N, S-CDs, metallic bonds are formed, leading to a static quenching effect (SQE). The electrostatic interaction between positive-charged cobalt complexes and negative-charged N, S-CDs induces the aggregation of metal complexes, increasing the particle size from 5.44 nm to 258.72 nm and resulting in a noticeable visual color change ultimately. N, S-CDs exhibited excellent potential for application in actual water samples, with the low detection limit (LOD) of 2.64 µM and 1.57 µM via fluorescence and colorimetric methods, respectively, and the recovery rate ranged from 94 % to 103 %. As far as we consider, the cross-referenced dual-mode platform proposed here may be an attractive strategy for the construction of high-performance sensors available for metal ion detection in actual aquatic environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence and Colorimetric
Dual-mode detection
Co(II)
Static quenching effect

Measurement of quantum yield (QY), lifetime and limit of detection
Detection of Co(II) with N, S-CDs
Pretreatment of samples for actual detection of Co(II)
Ultraviolet detection of Co(II) by N, S-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N, S-CDs’ HR-TEM image (a) (20 nm), (b) (10 nm); N, S-CDs’ diameter distribution histogram (c); N, S-CDs’ XPS diagrams of S 2p (d), N 1s (e); FTIR diagram of N, S-CDs (f).

N, S-CDs’ UV–vis spectra, emission and excitation curves. λex (excitation wavelength) = 370 nm, and λem (emission wavelength) = 560 nm. Inset: Images of N, S-CDs under the sunlight (left) and 370 nm UV lamp (right) (a); 3D FL spectrum (b); fluorescence spectrogram upon numerous excitation wavelengths (320–420 nm) (c); variation of N, S-CDs’ FL intensity during a week (d); FL intensity of N, S-CDs at different pH values (e); Absolute fluorescence quantum yield of N, S-CDs (f).

The anti-interference performance of N, S-CDs for various ions (100 µM) (a), and micro molecules (100 µM) (b) with and without Co(II) (100 µM) excited at 370 nm; FL spectra of N, S-CDs with Co(II) (0, 1, 6, 10, 16, 20, 24, 32, 36, 40 µM, Ex = 370 nm) (c); linear graph of Co(II) concentration (d).

The anti-interference performance of N, S-CDs for various ions (100 µM) (a), and micro molecules (100 µM) (b) in the presence or absence of Co(II) (100 µM) at the absorption wavelength of 420 nm; UV–vis spectroscopy of the N, S-CDs with Co(II) at 420 nm (0,1, 6, 10,16, 20, 24, 32, 36 µM) (c); linear graph of Co(II) concentration (d).

The FL lifetime spectra of N, S-CDs with and without Co(II) (a); the Stern-Volmer curves of N, S-CDs with Co(II) (b); the zeta potential of N, S-CDs, OPD-CDs, and the DTSA-CDs with and without Co(II) (c); UV–vis spectra of N, S-CDs, OPD-CDs, and the DTSA-CDs with and without Co(II) (d); TEM imagine of N, S-CDs with Co(II) (e); diameter distribution histogram of N, S-CDs with Co(II) (f); FTIR spectrum of N, S-CDs with and without Co(II) (g); XPS spectrum of N 1s (h) in N, S-CDs with and without Co(II); XPS spectrum of Co 2p in N, S-CDs with Co(II) (i).
Synthesis schematic of N, S-CDs and a dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric detection method for Co(II).
Possible mechanism of fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode detection for Co(II).
Detection of Co(II)in actual water samples.
Comparison of different methods for the sensing of Co2+.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescent and colorimetric dual-mode detection of Co(II) in actual water

College of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life and Environmental Sciences, Hunan University of Arts and Science, Changde, Hunan 415000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding Author at: College of Environment and Resources, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, Hunan 411105, PR China. Hunan Key Lab for Environmental Behavior of New Pollutants and Control Principle, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan, Hunan 411105, PR China. Tel.: +86 731 58298259; fax: +86 731 58298172.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focuses on the synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) using a hydrothermal method, and its application as a fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode probe for simple and rapid detection of Co(II). When Co(II) reacts with N, S-CDs, metallic bonds are formed, leading to a static quenching effect (SQE). The electrostatic interaction between positive-charged cobalt complexes and negative-charged N, S-CDs induces the aggregation of metal complexes, increasing the particle size from 5.44 nm to 258.72 nm and resulting in a noticeable visual color change ultimately. N, S-CDs exhibited excellent potential for application in actual water samples, with the low detection limit (LOD) of 2.64 µM and 1.57 µM via fluorescence and colorimetric methods, respectively, and the recovery rate ranged from 94 % to 103 %. As far as we consider, the cross-referenced dual-mode platform proposed here may be an attractive strategy for the construction of high-performance sensors available for metal ion detection in actual aquatic environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt is one of the essential trace elements in the human body and a crucial component of vitamin B12. However, excessive intake or persistent skin exposure to cobalt ions and compounds can affect the nervous system, leading to allergies, asthma, cardiovascular and cerebrovascular diseases, thyroid damage, or abnormal cell proliferation [1,2]. The International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) classifies Co(II) as a probable human carcinogen [3]. Unfortunately, significant amounts of cobalt, discharged from chlor-alkali, plastics, batteries, and electronics industries, remain in surface and groundwater in the form of Co(II) and CoCO3, accumulating in living organisms and human bodies through the food chain. Therefore, it is imperative to monitor and track cobalt levels in aquatic environments and to manage the risks that these contaminants may pose through long-term exposure to humans.

The detection of Co(II) has traditionally relied on electrochemical methods [4], inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [5], chemiluminescence [6], and atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) [7]. However, these traditional methods are limited in practical application due to the requirement for large instruments, high costs, complex pretreatment, and intricate operations. In contrast, fluorescence has garnered significant attention in metal sensing due to its simplicity, excellent sensitivity, and low cost. Recently, many fluorescent nanomaterials have been developed for the detection of Co(II). For example, Wu et al. developed NH2-carbon quantum dots (NH2-CDs) for cobalt ion fluorescence detection and cell imaging [8]. Sullam et al. designed a fluorescent test paper based on Si-QDs for rapid sensing of cobalt ions in water bodies [9]. Despite their high efficiency of these substances, these substances are prone to false positives due to the limited sample information provided by single-channel sensing. Challenges still exist in their application in real water environments due to fluctuations in instrument conditions and interferences from background signals [5]. Meanwhile, Zhang et al. adopted a label-free method based on inducing thiosulfate-stabilized gold nanoparticles to aggregate in the presence of ethylenediamine for colorimetric detection of Co(II) in aqueous solutions [10]. If fluorescence chemical measurements can be combined with visual colorimetry, recognizable also by the naked eye, more sample information may be conducive to enhance the accuracy of traditional single-channel sensing, and fluorescence/colorimetry dual-channel sensors have the potential to be utilized in a broader range of actual water environments. Thus, the selection of suitable materials for dual-mode sensing is particularly important.

Carbon dots (CDs), which are low-toxicity and low-cost fluorescence materials, find applications in various fields such as chemical sensing and catalysis. Additionally, more potential can be realized for their functionalization by the doping of heteroatoms like sulfur, boron, oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, etc. Among them, due to the high affinity of metal ions to heteroatoms, modification of carbon dots with sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) is considered to be an effective method to improve the electrochemical selectivity and sensitivity of metal ion sensors [11]. For instance, Crawford et al. designed a portable fiber to monitor Co(II) with nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) via fluorescence (FL) fluctuation [12]. Chen et al. synthesized nitrogen-sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) for FL sensing of Co(II) in the presence of cysteamine [13].

2,2′-Dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and ethanol originated from McLean Chemical Co., LTD. All metal salts (AgNO3, Ca(NO3)2, NiCl2, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, CrCl3, MnCl2, ZnSO4, CdCl2, MgSO4, CuSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, BaCl2, Pb(NO3)2, NaCl, HgCl2, SnCl2, KCl) and micro molecules (sucrose, vitamin B, ascorbic acid, glucose, riboflavin, triphosadenine, histidine) were acquired from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. The entire experiment was conducted with ultra-pure water. 500 Da dialysis membranes for material purification were manufactured through Shanggong Bioengineering Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China).
Instrument characterization is listed in Table S2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DTSA-CDs, OPD-CDs and N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, N, S-CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal reduction process. OPD served as the source of carbon and nitrogen, DTSA as the sulfur source, and anhydrous ethanol (≥99.5 %) and water as solvents. To begin, dissolve 0.0864 g of OPD and 0.0030 g DTSA in 20 mL ethanol. After 20 min of ultrasonic treatment, 30 mL of ultrapure water was added and thoroughly mixed. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon reactor and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the orange-brown product was filtered with 0.22 µM Millipore membranes to remove the large precipitates. With the aid of a dialysis membrane (500 Da), the filtered solution was dialyzed for 24 h. The dialysis solution was finally freeze-dried into a solid and preserved at 4℃ to obtain N, S-CDs powder.

Similarly, the synthesis methods for the OPD-CDs and DTSA-CDs followed the same procedures outlined above. Dissolve 0.0864 g of OPD in 20 mL anhydrous ethanol and 30 mL ultrapure water with an ultrasonic treatment for 20 min, and 0.0030 g DTSA was dissolved separately in another beaker. OPD-CDs and DTSA-CDs were obtained via the aforementioned operations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of quantum yield (QY), lifetime and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The average fluorescence lifetimes (Ex = 370 nm) were estimated by Eq.
(1)
[14].

τ =
A 1

The average lifetimes and the fitted fluorescence lifetime are denoted by theletters “τ” and“τ1 ” (“τ2 ”, “τ3 ”), respectively.“A1 ” (“A2 ”, “A3 ”) represents the equivalent proportion.

Eq.
(2) was employed to calculate the quantum yield in Fig. 2 f
Fig. S4
. [15].
(2)
Y X
=
Y S
F X
A S

X
2

A X

S
2
Fluorescence intensity, absorbance, refractive index, control substances and chemicals to be measured are denoted, respectively, by F, A, η, s and x.

The analysis curve was derived from average triplicate measurements. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated by Eq.
(3)
[14].
(3)
LOD = 3
∗
σ K
The standard deviation of the control and the slope of the analysis curve (analysis sensitivity) are denoted, respectively, by
σ
and K.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Co(II) with N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The study focused on investigating the interference rejection and linear relationship of the sensor for Co(II) in the dual-mode platform. The transformations in fluorescent intensities of N, S-CDs were registered with the incorporation of Co(II) (0–40 µM) excited at 370 nm [16]. Moreover, the colorimetric fluctuations of the solutions were assessed via a UV spectrophotometer at 420 nm, and photographs were snapped with a digital camera. To assess the selectivity of N, S-CDs, a series of micro molecules and metal ions (5 µL each, 10 mM) were combined with 100 µL N, S-CDs and 395 µL ultra-pure water, resulting in a total volume of 500 µL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of samples for actual detection of Co(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The lake water collected from Xiangtan University West Lake (Xiangtan, China) and the electroplating effluent from Shanshui steel structure were subjected to pretreatment before the actual detection of Co(II) [17]. The samples were spun up at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to get rid of large particles. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered via 0.22 µM Millipore membranes [18].

To attain superior optical characteristics, the temperature and time of preparation were initially improved. As depicted in Fig. S2
., OPD and DTSA were heated at 180 °C for 6 h to achieve the greatest fluorescence intensity. Briefly, N, S-CDs were generated upon the following conditions: 0.0864 g OPD, 20 mL ethanol, 0.0030 g DTSA, and 30 mL ultra-pure, all at 180 °C for 6 h.

To explore the morphology characterization and particle size distribution, HR-TEM image was displayed in Fig. 1 . The materials exist in shape of monodispersed sphere with an average diameter of 5.44 nm [19]. N, S-CDs possess a core–shell configuration with obvious graphitic carbon lattice fringes, which is similar to the structure of Fe3O4@Au@L-cysteine [20]. And a lattice spacing of 0.19 nm attributing to the (100) lattice spacing was observed in Fig. 1 a and c [21,22].The result implies the formation of the graphitic carbon core typical of o-phenylenediamine derived CDs [23].

Besides, the XPS, and FT-IR analyses were conducted to identify the components and functional groups of the core–shell structure of N, S-CDs. The prepared materials consist of four elements, C 1 s (62.94 %), O 1 s (24.39 %), N 1 s (8.18 %) and S 2p (4.49 %) through the full spectrum. The C 1 s spectral peaks were deconvoluted into three carbon bonds at 288.22, 284.80, 286.27 eV, corresponding to C = N/C = O, C = C/C − C, and C − N/C − O ( Fig. S1 ), respectively [24]. While the N 1 s spectral peak revealed the distinct peaks at 397.79 eV, 400.47 eV and 399.07 eV, indicating the presence of inorganic-N, pyrrolic N as well as pyridinic N, respectively [25,26]. The consequence indicates the carbon core of N, S-CDs originates from N-doped polyaromatic skeleton derived from the carbonation and condensation ofOPD precursors [27].Different from OPD, which is principally involved in constructing the carbon core, the other raw material DTSA contributes more to N, S-CDs’ surface condition. What is worth mentioning, C = O (531.68 eV) and C − O/O − H (532.45 eV) were found in the O 1 s spectrum, while C-S-C S2p3/2, SO3 2-, C − S − C S2p1/2 and SO4 2- were observed at 163.39, 168.03 164.56 and 169.25 eV in Fig. 1d, indicating the abundance of carboxyl groups, C − S − C and oxidized sulfur on the shell [28–31].

It is further elucidated that the weak peaks in Fig. 1 f at 2425, 1024, 753 and 616 cm−1 owing to the S − H bond, C − S bond, the C − H bending vibration of the benzene ring and the tensile vibration of the phenazine skeleton typically characterized by o-phenylenediamine derived CDs, respectively [32–34].Moreover, the stretching vibration bonds of C = O and N − H/O − H correspond to the peaks at 1631 and 3340 cm−1 in Fig. 1 f, respectively, confirming abundance of − NH2 and − COOH on the shell [23,35]. The vibrations of C = C, C − O, C − N, and C − C were discovered at 1530, 1133, 1384, and 1024 cm−1 [36,37]. The phenomena indicates that the carbon core is assumed to originate from the carbonization of OPD-derived π-conjugated domains and the shell consists of a mass of functional groups, such as − COOH, −NH2, C − S, S − S and S − H, which are residues from OPD and DTSA [23].

Additionally, the optical traits were measured using ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometers and fluorescence photometers. N, S-CDs appears bright yellow under 370 nm UV light and yellow-orange under sunlight. In Fig. 2
a, the UV–vis absorption bands between 200 and 400 nm typically correspond to π → π* transitions of C = C/C − C bond in isolated sp2 carbon domains of a graphene-like carbon lattice with defects as well as n → π* transitions of aromatic systems containing abundant C − N/C = N or C − O/C = O [38]. The peak at 420 nm is mainly attributed to conjugated structural functional groups between the phenazine ring structure and –NH2, indicating the generation of DAP (2,3-Diaminophenazine) [39]. The fluorescence (FL) spectrum exhibits the maximum emission at 560 nm when excited at 370 nm (Fig. 2 b). Furthermore, there is no dependence between the emission (Em) wavelength and excitation (Ex) wavelength [40]. It can be seen from Fig. 2e that there is no significant change in fluorescence intensity in the pH range from 5 to 9, which is applicable to the detection of treated electroplating wastewater (pH = 6 ∼ 9). The sensor demonstrates excellent FL stability in Fig. S3 . The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of N, S-CDs is 4.45 % in Fig. 2 f. The photoluminescence (PL) traits are comparable to those of electrically generated carbon dots from graphite, due to the precise size regularity and similar surface conditions of sp2 clusters [41].

In order to explore the specific selectivity for ions, the FL intensity of Co2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Ba2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni+ was acquired at 370 nm (Ex) [42]. It was discovered that only the presence of Co(II) caused a considerable drop in FL intensity, while the involvement of other ions had little impact on fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3
a and c). The specific selectivity for micro molecules was conducted by the same way (Fig. 3 b). Subsequently, the sensitivity towards Co(II) was explored based on the specific reactivity of the detector. The FL intensity of the sensor steadily diminishes with the increase of Co(II) concentration, which established the remarkable sensitivity of concentration dependent fluorescence quenching. Through multiple tests and calculate Ln F, it showed an excellent linear connection with Co(II) content in the scope of 0–40 µM, yielding a linear coefficient R2 = 0.9944 (Fig. 3 d). The LOD is estimated to be 2.64 µM upon 3 times standard deviation [43], far lower than the national standard’s highest limit for wastewater discharge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultraviolet detection of Co(II) by N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the specific selectivity for metal cations, the UV absorption intensity was recorded at the absorption wavelength of 420 nm, including Co2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Sn2+, Ba2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni+. It was found that the presence of only Co(II) yielded a significant rise in the UV absorbance, while the addition of other ions had no impact on the absorption (Fig. 4
a and c). Subsequently, the sensitivity to Co(II) was assessed upon the specific selectivity.The materials’ specific selectivity for micro molecules was conducted similarly. The UV absorbance of the sensor steadily declines with the increase of Co(II) concentration, demonstrating a remarkable sensitivity of concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching.After conducting multiple tests to calculate the Abs, a strong linear correlation with Co(II) concentration ranging from 0 to 36 µM was observed, yielding a linear regression of R2 = 0.9935. The detection limit is calculated to be 1.57 µM upon 3 times standard deviation [43], far lower than the national standard’s highest limit for wastewater discharge.

We also contrasted the analytical performance of this dual-mode assay with that of other reported Co(II) sensors that have been described in Table 2.The N, S-CDs in this job exhibited decent performance as anticipated, substantially minimizing the interference of analyte independent factors in comparison with single-mode strategies.

In consideration of the interactions between the CDs and targets, the FL quenching theories primarily encompass five distinct categories: the dynamic quenching effect (DQE), photoinduced electron transfer(PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), static quenching effect (SQE), inner filter effect (IFE), and transmission of surface energy (SET) [44]. To comprehensively explore the FL quenching principle of Co(II) to N, S-CDs, a range of calculations and experimentations were undertaken.

The Fig. 5
a illustrated the FL lifetime of N, S-CDs. In accordance to the double exponential fluorescence attenuation formula, the FL lifetimes of N, S-CDs in the inexistence and existence of Co(II) were 2.30 ns and 2.23 ns, respectively. The distance in both dispersions was too weak to be noticed, suggesting SQE approximately involved in the quenching [45]. To determine whether the interaction between the quenching agents and the fluorophores is static quenching or dynamic quenching, the Stern-Volmer (SV) formula in Eq.
(4) provides a theoretically explanation for the FL quenching mechanism and serves as a basis for further research [46,47].
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
∙ C
(5)
K
SV
=
K q
∙
τ 0

The symbols F0 , F, C, KSV , Kq and
τ 0
stand for the FL intensity in the inexistence and existence of Co(II), the content of Co(II), the SV quenching constant, FL quenching rate constant, and the FL lifetime in the formula, respectively.
Thus, the linear connection between the FL intensity ratio (F0/F) and the cobalt ion content is available to be expressed as follows (Fig. 5 b).

In brief, the value of τ0 , KSV and Kq are calculated to be 2.30 ns, 7.11 × 104 M−1 and 3.09 × 1012 L/(mol∙s). A calculated Kq is vastly larger than the Maximum collision quenching constant 2.0 × 1010 L/(mol∙s), further confirming that SQE is involved in the FL quenching theory [48].

Meanwhile, the core–shell structure also affects the dual-mode sensing to a certain degree. It is obvious that the UV–vis absorption peak at 420 nm markedly increased with the presence of Co(II). As previously discussed, the peak at 420 nm represented the phenazine structure, which is the primary component of the carbon core. According to the phenomena, we speculated the electrostatic attraction between Co(II) and N, S-CDs accelerates the formation of carbon nuclei. Considering the component of the materials, controlled experimentations were provided to confirm the role OPD and DTSA play in the dual-mode sensing. Therefore, OPD-CDs and DTSA-CDs were synthesized by OPD and DTSA separately. The solution of OPD-CDs appeared yellow-orange and showed yellow fluorescence excited at 370 nm wavelength similar to N, S-CDs. In addition, the presence of Co(II) in OPD-CDs resulted in a darker color, and the fluorescence was quenched and zeta potential enhanced from 8.06 mV to 25.6 mV (Fig. 5 c). The latter was distinct from the negative potential of N, S-CDs, further implying the involvement of DTSA provided carboxyl group and sulfhydryl to the N, S-CDs’ surface. However, the changes in FL intensity, UV–visible wavelength absorption and zeta potential with and without Co(II) were less obvious in OPD-CDs compared to N, S-CDs ( Fig. S5 ). The electrostatic attraction betweenCo(II) and negative charges of the N, S-CDs’ surface reinforces this attachment better than OPD-CDs [49]. DTSA-CDs exhibited transparent with a blue-fluoresce peak when excited at 370 nm wavelength. Different from the disparity of OPD-CDs, the gap of DTSA-CDs with and without Co(II) was too weak to be noted. The phenomena revealed that OPD-CDs are primarily responsible for the colorimetric phenomenon and fluorescence quenching induced by Co(II), whereas the addition of DTSA as sulfur source aggravates the phenomenon. Moreover, the interaction with cobalt may cause N, S-CDs to aggregate into complexes with darker colors and FL quenching. It is illustrated the average diameter of N, S-CDs increased from 5.44 nm to 258.72 nm and the surface shell of the carbon core becomes indistinguishable with the addition of Co(II) in the TEM images (Fig. 5 e and f). The phenomena demonstrated collapse of the N, S-CDs’ surface and the formation of aggregates due to dehydration, carbonization and electrostatic attraction [39,50].

On the other hand, the mechanisms of dual-mode detection were further verified through the combination of XPS, Zeta, and FT-IR results. The peaks of N, S-CDs at 1583 and 1016 cm−1 disappeared while the peak at 1524 cm−1 occurred after the addition of Co(II) in the FT-IR spectra, indicating the merging of Co(II) with amine and C − S (Fig. 5 g). Meanwhile, the N 1s peak also displayed that the pyrrole nitrogen diminished while the pyridine nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen and Co − N eased (Fig. 5 h). Generally, the pyrrole nitrogen as the electron-rich structure transformed into the pyridine nitrogen as the electron-deficient system, indicating the oxidation of the pyrrole nitrogen consistent with the result of Zeta potential [50]. What’s more, the formation of graphitic nitrogen and Co − N indicates further dehydration carbonization and the nitrogen reduction. In addition, Co2p peaks in Fig. 5 i were deconvoluted into four main bands at 781.34, 784.16, 796.66 and 798.73 eV, corresponding to Co(III) and Co(II) of Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2, respectively [51]. This phenomenon indicates that Co(II) was partially oxidized to Co(III) and that S-S upon the N, S-CDs’ surface serves as an active site for hydroxyl group. Co(III) species may bind to pyridine nitrogen and S-S. The zeta potential of the N, S-CDs raised from –3.48 to 26.96 mV since Co(II) was introduced. The result revealed that the initial negative charge on the surface of N, S-CDs attracts cobalt for oxidation, and protonation occurs due to the merging of cobalt with nitrogen or S-S groups [52,53]. Considering the investigations above, we hypothesize that the pyrrole nitrogen, C − S − C (S2p3/2), S − S, and − OH at the N, S-CDs’ face collaborate in the dehydration carbonization and the oxidation of Co(II) to Co(III), where Co(III) is merged with S − S, the pyridine nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen and − NH2 on N, S-CDs [54].

Considering the aforementioned descriptions, Scheme 2
. proposes a potential mechanism for fluorescence quenching and colorimetric phenomenon. The pyrrole nitrogen on the shells undergoes oxidation to pyridine nitrogen and reduction to graphitic nitrogen and Co − N. Additionally, SO3 2- is reduced to C − S − C ( Table S1
.). Concurrently, Co(II) undergoes oxidation to Co(III) and combines with the negative-potential shells to form cobalt complex with positive potential, which attracted more carbon dots to aggregate. Upon reaching a potential of 25 mV, a stable, non-fluorescent, and brown chelate was formed.

Besides, a comparison with other methods for detecting Co(II) is presented in Table 2 . N, S-CDs in this work had a low detection limit, intuition and fewer false positives, which can be applied in high-sensitivity sensing of Co(II) in wastewater at low concentrations. Expanding the linear range and the applicable pH range can help alleviate the limitations of its application.

In this study, a novel N, S-CDs was prepared by one-pot hydrothermal procedure. The N, S-CDs exhibit excellent chemical properties and FL stability. The dual-mode detection approach utilizing N, S-CDs as the probe achieved a fluorescence LOD of 2.64 µM and a colorimetric LOD of 1.57 µM, respectively. What’s more, the proposed core–shell structure elucidates the formation of CDs and their interaction with quenchers and provides a model for studying other dual-mode detection methods from a microscopic morphology perspective. Finally, the developed dual-mode sensor is not only apt for detecting Co(II) in actual water environments, but also has the application potential for integration with commercially available portable devices (e.g., microfluidic chips and paper-based sensors) and developing multi-functional facilities (e.g., All-In-One photoelectrochemical system combining detection, removal, and recovery of contaminants in a single process, as well as dual mode secure color-changing ink for information encryption and fingerprint recognition). Coupled with visual app software, the dual-mode technology could pave the way for real-time online monitoring and intelligent sensor analysis in various application fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20617), Hunan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2022JJ50131, 2022JJ30559, 2021JJ30664), and Hunan Provincial University Key Laboratory for Environmental and Ecological Health.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2024.02.053.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
